content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by A-97-579(Cronin)

August 13, 1991

Arnold M. Glasman
The City of Pomona
City Hall, 505 So. Carey Avenue
Box 660
Pomona, CA  91769

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-91-345

Dear Mr. Glasman:

     You have requested advice on behalf of the City of Pomona
Vice Mayor Nell Soto and Councilmember Tomas Ursua concerning the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

     Since our advice is limited only to provisions of the Act, we
make no comment regarding any other conflict-of-interest laws.
Furthermore, our advice is prospective in nature and we do not
comment or advise regarding past conduct.

QUESTION

     Vice Mayor Soto and Councilmember Ursua are named defendants
in a lawsuit.  Under the Act, may they participate in a
governmental decision regarding their requests for the city to
provide them with a legal defense?

CONCLUSION

     Vice Mayor Soto and Councilmember Ursua may participate in a
decision regarding their requests for the city to provide them
with a legal defense as long as payment of attorneys' fees is
considered either compensation or terms and conditions of office.
Generally, when a governmental entity pays for the attorneys' fees

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-345
Page 2


of its officers or employees for acts within the scope of their
employment, the payment is in the nature of compensation if the
payment of the attorneys' fees is authorized by law.

FACTS

A civil complaint, filed in the Superior Court of Los Angeles
County, named Tomas Ursua and Nell Soto as defendants and alleged
that they made disparaging remarks which caused the plaintiffs
injury. These remarks revolve around a report, prepared by the
firm of Saranow, Wells & Emirhanian, which was authorized by the
city in order to investigate alleged improprieties by former city
staff. This confidential report was distributed to the city
council. A private citizen obtained possession of the report and
released it to the general public and the local newspapers.

The complaint alleges that Councilmember Ursua made
statements to reporters from the Inland Valley Daily Bulletin and
the Los Angeles Times concerning the report. Mr. Ursua and other
councilmembers were contacted by newspaper reporters who were
preparing a story concerning the issue.

The complaint further alleges that Vice Mayor Soto made
several statements at a candidates' forum, which was sponsored by
the Pomona Chamber of Commerce and held in conjunction with the
campaign for the April, 1991 city council election. Vice Mayor
Soto, who was introduced as the city's vice mayor and referred to
by her office during the forum, is alleged to have made several
statements alluding to the investigation and the report.

The issue of the city providing a legal defense was discussed
and voted upon at the June 11, 1991 city council meeting. The
council determined that both Vice Mayor Soto and Councilmember
Ursua were acting outside the course and scope of their employment
and voted against providing a defense in this matter. Upon your
recommendation, neither Ms. Soto nor Mr. Ursua participated in the
closed session discussion or the vote concerning their defense.
Both councilmembers have requested that the FPPC evaluate their
ability to now participate in this decision.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they have a financial interest. An
official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning
of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on the official, on a member of
the official's immediate family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Our File No. A-91-345
Page 3


(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

Section 87103.

The effect of a governmental decision is material if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets
(other than interest in real property), or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing
by at least $250. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).) For purposes of
this analysis, we will assume that the cost of each
councilmember's legal representation in this matter will exceed
$250. Accordingly, the effect of a decision to provide a legal
defense would be considered material in that it will increase or
decrease the assets or liabilities of each councilmember by $250
or more.

The next determination is whether participating in a decision
to provide the councilmembers with a legal defense constitutes the
making of a governmental decision. Regulation 18700(d) provides
that making or participating in the making of a governmental
decision shall not include:

Actions by public officials, employees, or
employee representatives relating to their
compensation or the terms or conditions of their
employment or contract.

Regulation 18700(d)(3).

Our File No. A-91-345
Page 4

The Commission has also determined by regulation that an
official is using to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision of an agency if the official:

Negotiates his or her compensation or the terms and
conditions of his or her employment or contract.

Regulation 18700.1(b)(3).

The Commission has determined that it is necessary that
public officials be permitted to make and participate in making
decisions affecting their own compensation and the terms and
conditions of their own employment or contract. (Shectman Advice
Letter, No. A-87-226.) Accordingly, if providing the
councilmembers with a defense is a component of the terms and
conditions of their employment with the city, they may participate
in decisions regarding whether the city should provide them with a
legal defense.

We have previously advised that councilmembers may
participate in and vote on matters concerning their fringe
benefits when the fringe benefits are considered to be a part of
the employee's compensation. (Flitner Advice Letter, No. A-87-
096.)

If any statute, ordinance or charter provision permits the
payment of an official's attorneys' fees for the defense of a
legal matter relating to acts within the scope of the official's
public employment, we will consider the payments to be in the
nature of compensation from the city. In such cases, the public
official involved in the legal action may participate in decisions
regarding whether the public entity will provide him or her with a
defense.

However, in circumstances like those presented here, if one
councilmember is disqualified from participating in a decision
whether the city should pay for his or her own defense, he or she
is also disqualified from participating in a decision regarding
the defense of another councilmember. We reach this conclusion
have a material financial effect on both councilmembers. Once the
city council determines to provide or to deny a defense to one
councilmember, it is unlikely that it will reach a different

2
Note, however, that payments of punitive damage judgments
against public officials in civil cases which concern acts within
the official's employment are not considered to be in the nature
of compensation from the public entity for purposes of the Act.
(Section 825(d); Smith Advice Letter, No. A-87-305.)

Our File No. A-91-345
Page 5


conclusion with respect to the other councilmember. Therefore, it
is reasonably foreseeable that any decision regarding the legal
defense will affect the financial interests of both
councilmembers. Accordingly, a disqualified councilmember may not
participate in such decisions unless his or her participation is
legally required to obtain a quorum.

     We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.³

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        Jill Stecher
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JS:dg




³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
